ORDER RE: MOTION TO SET ASIDE STAY AND SET MATTER FOR JURY TRIAL WITH PRIORITY OVER ALL OTHER CIVIL CASES

LAW AND MOTION 302, CLAIMANT EVA SCALLA'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE STAY AND SET CASE FOR PRIORITY CIVIL JURY TRIAL CLAIMANT EVA SCALLA'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE STAY IS TREATED AS A MOTION TO LIFT THE STAY EFFECTED BY THE STIPULATED ORDER FILED APRIL 28, 2017 AND, AS SO TREATED, IT IS GRANTED DUE TO NO OPPOSITION BY THE PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA. TO THE EXTENT THE MOTION SEEKS TO VACATE THE ORDER FILED APRIL 28, 2017, THAT PORTION OF THE MOTION IS DENIED BECAUSE THERE IS NO BASIS TO VACATE THAT ORDER. THE PORTION OF THE MOTION REQUESTING THAT THE CASE BE SET FOR A JURY TRIAL ON A PRIORITY BASIS IS TAKEN OFF CALENDAR WITHOUT PREJUDICE TO BEING REFILED TO BE HEARD IN DEPARTMENT 206, WHICH IS THE DEPARTMENT THAT HEARS MOTIONS TO SET TRIALS ON A PRIORITY BASIS. ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, NOT REPORTED. =(302/HK)

DAVID MICHAEL, ESQ. APPEARING FOR CLAIMANT. AFTER ARGUMENT, THE COURT GRANTS CLAIMANT'S MOTION TO SET CASE FOR PRIORITY JURY TRIAL. THE TRIAL DATE IS SET FOR 6/17/19 AT 9:30AM IN DEPT. 206 AS A PRIORITY CIVIL JURY TRIAL. ANY NEW DISCOVERY AND DISPOSITIVE MOTIONS SHALL TRACK THE NEW TRIAL DATE. ALL MOTIONS RELATED TO PRIOR DISCOVERY SHALL BE HEARD IN LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302. Motion To Set Case For Priority Civil Jury Trial
Off Calendar Dec-04-2018 Text Ruling

Motion To Set Aside Stay And Set Case For Priority Civil Jury Trial
Off Calendar Oct-24-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CLAIMANT EVA SCALLA'S Motion To Set Aside Stay And Set Case For Priority Civil Jury Trial.

Claimant Eva Scalla's motion to set aside stay is treated as a motion to lift the stay effected by the stipulated order filed April 28, 2017 and, as so treated, it is granted due to no opposition by the petitioner People of the State of California. To the extent the motion seeks to vacate the order filed April 28, 2017, that portion of the motion is denied because there is no basis to vacate that order. The portion of the motion requesting that the case be set for a jury trial on a priority basis is taken off calendar without prejudice to being refiled to be heard in department 206, which is the department that hears motions to set trials on a priority basis.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, Maria.Torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/HK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Search Warrant And Suppress Evidence_1226372-01
Off Calendar Jul-22-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Edward Michael Burch of Law Offices of David Michael (415-946-8996) appears for respondent/claimant, Eva Scalla

No other appearance is made in this matter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

RESPONDENT/CLAIMANT EVA SCALLA'S Motion To Quash Search Warrant And Suppress Evidence is DENIED. Assuming arguendo that the exclusionary rule could apply and claimant Eva Scalla may seek suppression in this court, probable cause existed for the search warrant. Recorded telephone calls between Scalla and her jailed son strongly indicated she was an accomplice in illegal drug activities. Thus, there was "a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime" would "be found in" Scalla's residence. (See Illinois v. Gates (1983) 462 U.S. 213, 238.)
Assuming arguendo that Scalla has standing to challenge the warrant in this court and to seek to traverse it, the police affidavit could not have misled the judge who issued the search warrant. (See People v. Lieng (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 1213, 1229.) The affidavit was detailed and thorough; it contained no false statements. Fairly read, the quoted transcript of a jail call indicates that Scalla knew in advance of a "secret" place where her son kept a drug prescription pad - an item he was relieved to learn had not been seized in a police search of his residence. In any event, this temporal distinction is not material or substantial.
Finally, Scalla's papers do not account for U.S. Supreme Court holdings that evidence gathered in violation of the Fourth Amendment can be excluded only if police were deliberate, reckless or grossly negligent - not merely negligent. (Herring v. U.S. (2009) 129 S.Ct. 695; Davis v. U.S. (2011) 131 S.Ct. 2419.) No such showing has been made here. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Richard Ulmer; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Reyes Hunter, CSR #6576, Bus. Tel: 530-339-5023. (D302)
